Empires & Puzzles Bramble Review: The Gentle Faun Gardener

Bramble brings a basket of veggies and a unique mana mechanic to Empires & Puzzles. Is his 'safe cleanse' and delayed mana worth the roster spot in today's fast-paced meta? We dig into the stats and strategy.
Bramble Review: The Faun Who Plants Mana Seeds (And Waits 3 Turns)
Bramble: The Gentle Faun Gardener Who Plants Mana Seeds for Later
Bramble is a 5-star Nature (Green) hero from the

family, released in early 2025. He fits the classic archetype of a sturdy support healer but introduces a twist with his mana generation mechanic. Unlike the instant gratification of older meta healers, Bramble plants a "mana seed" that blooms after three turns—or immediately if someone tries to weed it out. While his stats have aged slightly over the last year, his kit offers specific utility that keeps him relevant, particularly for players dealing with heavy ailment teams or specific War rules.

Bramble’s artwork is a delightful departure from the usual "armored warrior screaming at the sky." He’s a gentle, bearded faun carrying a woven basket overflowing with oversized vegetables (is that a prize-winning turnip?) and a cute rabbit. He has massive, curling ram horns adorned with flowers and a little robin perched on his finger. It’s giving "Disney Princess but make it a Satyr" energy. He looks like he’d offer you a carrot soup before casually cleansing your entire team of necrotic poison.
As of early 2026, Bramble's stats show the signs of being a year old. His
is noticeably behind the latest releases which are pushing 1260+.
Low by modern standards, but irrelevant for his primary role as a healer.
His health pool is surprisingly robust and still competitive, but his defense stat is lagging behind the ~1300+ standard of new tank-grade heroes. He relies heavily on his healing to stay relevant.

The -50/60% accuracy on minions is a hidden stat that effectively reduces incoming damage from minion-heavy teams by half.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Woodland Faun family and Bramble's passives are where much of his lingering value lies.
The resistance to mana reduction is excellent. In a meta full of mana cutters and controllers, having a 75-100% chance to say "no" to mana cuts ensures he can fire his heal when needed.
This is arguably his best feature. Making enemy minions and Mega Minions miss 50-60% of their shots massively increases team survivability against summoner-heavy defenses.
Immunity to Defense Down (and Wither) makes him much tankier than his stats suggest. He won't get softened up for a sniper kill.
Extremely niche, mostly relevant for "Insanity" wars or countering specific Cthulhu-mythos themed heroes.
is a decent defensive power, giving him some self-sustain, but it's generally considered less impactful than "Revive" or "Defense Up" for a healer.
Upon release, Bramble was seen as a "fair" Xnolphod successor—bringing back mana generation but with a delay to prevent the infinite mana loops that plagued the game previously.
His stats have fallen off. While 2200 HP is fine, his defense is soft. The lack of
health is his biggest aging factor; modern healers almost exclusively offer overhealing to counter max-health reduction teams.
. He is no longer a top-tier defense hero. However, he remains a strong offensive tool in
(where his mana buff gets cleansed and triggers instantly) and against heavy minion teams thanks to his passive.
As of this review, Bramble does not have a costume.
Bramble is a Druid, and his job is to survive and heal.

His passive makes enemy minions significantly less threatening without him doing anything active.

Bramble shines when you manipulate his mana buff.
In Equalizer wars, fire Bramble right before the equalizer triggers. The war effect removes the buff, granting everyone instant mana to fire again next turn.
Bramble + Heroes with self-ailments (e.g., Killhare, Lepus).
Bramble’s safe cleanse can remove the negative defense ailments these heroes cast on themselves without penalty.

If you are blocked, Bramble can't place the mana seed.

If you pulled Bramble early, rejoice! A 50% healer will carry you through almost all map stages and early raid tiers. Focus on keeping him alive. Don't worry about the complex mana timing yet; just treat him as a big heal button.

Bramble is a niche tool for your War bench. He shouldn't be your primary healer for general raiding due to the lack of overheal. However, keep him prepped for Equalizer wars and as a counter-pick into heavy minion defenses where his passive value is immense. Do not prioritize Alpha Aethers unless you really love the art.
The community had mixed feelings upon his release.
Many players compared him to the pre-nerf Xnolphod, noting,
"He feels like he's missing an extra skill compared to modern heroes,"
but appreciated the return of a 20%+ mana boost.
Opinions were split! Some called it
while others found the bird interaction
Beta testers noted he originally had a priority dispel for 1 ailment, but this was changed to a full "safe cleanse" on release—a change generally seen as a buff for versatility.

Bramble is a hero who requires patience—both in his mechanic and in finding the right battle to use him. He isn't the flashy, game-breaking monster that instant-win heroes are, but he is a reliable, safe, and strategic gardener. He plants the seeds of victory, and if you can survive three turns (or play the War rules right), you'll reap a bountiful harvest.
A solid B-tier hero in general play, rising to A-tier in Equalizer Wars. Keep him for the rainy days.
